Summary: Systematic elaboration present. In ER+ breast cancer, ESR1 amplifications and in‑frame fusions occur and fusions have been reported in at least 1% of metastatic tumors, often in endocrine‑resistant disease. Most ESR1 fusions retain the N‑terminal DNA‑binding domain and exclude the ligand‑binding domain. These fusion proteins drive estrogen‑independent ER transcriptional activity, cell proliferation, motility, and metastasis. Cells and a patient‑derived xenograft with ESR1 fusions remained sensitive to CDK4/6 inhibition and the authors suggest adding CDK4/6 inhibitors to adjuvant endocrine therapy may prevent fusion acquisition. ESR1 ligand‑binding‑domain mutant Y537S drives estrogen‑independent metabolic rewiring. ESR1 Y537S increases tricarboxylic acid cycle activity, confers glucose independence, and increases reliance on glutamine. Antiestrogens suppress ER and induce a metabolically dormant, self‑renewal‑deficient state. Chronic fulvestrant increases paracrine IL‑6 and IL‑6 elevates oxidative phosphorylation to restore growth in quiescent CD133hi/ERlo/Mito lo cells in an ER‑independent manner. Proteolysis‑targeting chimeras bind a region shared by mutant and wild‑type ER and recruit an E3 ubiquitin ligase to degrade ER. ARV‑471 degrades Y537S and D538G and inhibits tumor growth in a patient‑derived xenograft harboring ESR1 Y537S, and the authors speculate that targeting both mutant and wild‑type ER earlier may prevent acquisition of LBD mutations.
References: Hanker, A. B., Sudhan, D. R., & Arteaga, C. L. (2020). Overcoming Endocrine Resistance in Breast Cancer. Cancer Cell, 37(4), 496-513. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ccell.2020.03.009
